OK, I’m an hour and 15 minutes into this video now. I am a sailor and I have steamed across the Atlantic ocean, Mediterranean, Sea, and Indian ocean on a large modern metal ship in the Navy; I have also traveled on smaller craft with wood hulls and sails along the coast of North America and up rivers, and I can tell you that current, Wind, time of year, and weather all play a huge part in how long it takes you to get anywhere. 

I also have an academian friend who believes that the people of Jared did in fact cross the Pacific Ocean and landed on what is now the West Coast of North America. he and I have talked many times and we also believe that the original group that landed there separated into at least two other groups over a period of time. 

One of those groups headed east into North America another headed north where they met other people and the third headed south where they met other people further down in central and south America as we know them now. that’s what explain a number of cultural similarities and anomalies as well as some interesting genetics. 

Getting back to the sailing, the wind is your friend when it is at your back and it is a nightmare when it is cutting across you or blowing against your bow when you are at sea, or even on a large body of water inland. I have studied the evolution of boats and ships going all the way back to the Sumerian culture. Did you know that boats (barges) back in that day were made out of tightly bundled groups of reeds bound together with oiled cordage, and then bound together again with the bow and stern gathered together into a point and curved upwards? this was done for a reason and it used the strength and flexibility of the building materials as a strong point. 

The craft were steered by a set of two blade paddles made of a lightweight wood, which were easy to manipulate, but heavy enough to stay down in the water and steer if they were fixed towards the rear of the craft. They looked like fins on a turtle sticking down into the water. The reed boats will only last for so long before they become waterlogged through , and the boat or barge begins to take on water and sink. They are excellent for traveling on rivers, lakes, and shallow or calmer water because of their buoyancy and maneuverability, and their ability to carry a significant amount of weight which you would never guessed by looking at them.  

If you are sailing across a body of water which has a little bit more salt content it, it would add buoyancy and it would allow The bundles of reeds to not take on so much water but the salt is very caustic overtime on natural materials so if they are not sealed or preserved in someway, the salt will actually get into it and weigh it down as well. Salt is very caustic on anything made of metal and it will overtime removed natural oils from wood or plant matter  that will then allow the water to cause wood or biological materials to start to decay and breakdown. They have already proven that building this kind of boat or ship would not be a wise idea for a transatlantic or trans Pacific voyage due to the same reason. 

Thor Heyerdahl met this problem in the Ra reed boat expedition many years ago. So it makes sense that you would want to build a boat or marine vessel out of something much sturdier. I have looked into the possibility of a combination of wood and bamboo which makes for an intriguing combination of durability and flexibility for someone who is not well-versed in shipbuilding, but already knows the basic techniques.

I'd like to make a clarification on the antecedent portion of the video. An antecedent, correctly defined as "a word, phrase, clause, or sentence to which another word refers." However, I just feel like a small yet important detail was missed when explaining why the relative clause, "which was on the east side of the river Sidon", refers to the south wilderness and not the borders of Manti. Yes, the "south wilderness" would be the antecedent in this sentence because it is closest to the relative clause. This tendency to use proximity in this sort of situation is called late/low attachment, which English heavily prefers. Some languages prefer early/high attachment. Spanish, French, German, Dutch, Japanese, or Russian would interpret the relative clause as modifying the main/first noun phrase and the antecedent is suddenly farther away.

I really appreciate the unique, physical-geography approach in these videos, but when you lay the Baja model strictly against the actual text of the Book of Mormon and environmental science, there are a few massive holes:

The Missing "Sea North": Helaman 11:20 explicitly borders the land with four seas (North, South, East, West). A peninsula fundamentally lacks a Sea North, which breaks the macro-geography of the text.

The 8-Year Farming Trek: The "progressive seed adaptation" theory contradicts 1 Nephi 17:2 & 12, which states they survived on "raw meat" and didn't even cook their food. They relied entirely on direct divine intervention to survive the wilderness, not multi-year desert farming.

The River Sidon Paradox: The Baja model relies on occasional "flash floods" to wash bodies to the sea (Alma 3:3). However, the text describes a permanent river where armies safely camp on the banks (Alma 2:34) and cross on foot. Furthermore, Alma 22:27 says the head of the river flows "from the east towards the west," contradicting the idea that the headwaters spring up in the middle of Zarahemla.

The "Ancient" Olive & Date Trees: The 2010 University of Arizona study cited in the first video explicitly attributes the Old World seeds in Baja to 18th-century Spanish Jesuit missionaries. There is absolutely zero Old World pollen or ancient agricultural artifacts in Baja from 600 BC.

Desert Carrying Capacity: To make Baja work, you have to drastically shrink the population size because a desert simply doesn't have the water or caloric output to sustain an empire. Doing this forces you to ignore the literal text of the book, which numbers casualties in the tens of thousands (Mormon 6) and millions (Ether 15).

It's a really interesting thought experiment, but it ultimately requires ignoring explicit scriptures and established botany to make the map fit.

Cow and ox are basically redundant, goat and wild goat somewhat too.  We ought to extend our imagination to think about what Nephi might have actually been engraving on his plates that the translator chose to render in those English words.  We should keep in mind that the translator:
preserved the Egyptian and Hebrew grammar patterns,
chose to use the word "brass" instead of "bronze" for what would have most likely been a copper-tin alloy (or maybe even a variety of tumbaga?), but probably not copper-zinc,
chose to leave Mormon's "dragons" instead of offering a recognizable word (though it's possible Mormon took the word for a mythological creature based on his reading of Isaiah),
chose to leave ziff, neas, sheum, curelom, and cumom untranslated.  It could have chosen English words from the 19th century in a sort of omniscient override, but it didn't.  It kept closer to the perspective and the linguistic character -- including limitations -- of the source.
So I think we can expect it to do the same where these animal words are concerned: these are words chosen by an immigrant encountering native fauna and choosing the words he knows that best fit them.  We shouldn't insist on _bos taurus_ (especially not with two redundant words for the same species), _equus asinus_ (and note the genus name meaning "horse"!), _equus ferrus,_ _capra hircus,_ or _capra aegagrus_ being present in Baja in 580 BC.

Ancient Midwest roads:

Key ancient and historic trails in the Midwest include:
US-12 Heritage Trail / Sauk Trail (Michigan): Stretching over 200 miles from Detroit to New Buffalo, this route was originally a vast network of Native American trails. It was a primary migration path for pioneers and eventually evolved into a major motor route.
The Great Hopewell Road (Ohio): Dating back to ancient Native American earthwork builders (the Hopewell Culture), this is theorized to be an ancient 60-mile ceremonial road linking the Newark Earthworks to Chillicothe. Its straight-line route is still visible in places through archaeological imaging.
Three Notch Road (Missouri): Located near Mine La Motte and Ste. Genevieve, this is recognized as the oldest road in Missouri. Established roughly 300 years ago, Native Americans and French-Canadian explorers marked the trees with three notches to guide travelers.
Chicago's Natural Ridges (Illinois): Long before the modern grid system, ancient tribes and French trappers navigated the Chicago area via the dry ridges created by retreating glaciers. Major modern streets like Ridge Avenue and Milwaukee Avenue sit directly atop these centuries-old indigenous pathways.
The Great River Road (Minnesota to Illinois): Tracing the path of the Mississippi River, segments of this scenic route were mapped as early as 1763 but served as indigenous trails for thousands of years as Native peoples moved along the high riverbanks.

That’s not true and has been debunked. 

On 2 July 1839, Joseph Smith and other church leaders visited the site [of "Zarahemla, across the river from Nauvoo] in question. The entry published in the History of the Church reads as follows:
Spent the forenoon of this day on the Iowa side of the river. Went, in company with Elders Sidney Rigdon, Hyrum Smith, and Bishops Whitney and Knight, and others, to visit a purchase lately made by Bishop Knight as a location for a town, and advised that a town be built there, and called Zarahemla.[3]
The last three words of this entry, "and called Zarahemla," were not written by Joseph Smith but were written into the "Manuscript History of Joseph Smith" by Elder Willard Richards when he recorded the history for that date sometime after the Prophet's death in 1844.[4] However, referring to the settlement as "Zarahemla" before the March 1841 revelation is consistent with other historical evidence showing that the Saints already referred to the site by that name. Brigham Young, who began keeping a regular journal in early 1839, recorded that on 2 July 1839 "Brothers Joseph, Hyrum and others came over the river to Montrose, and went out on the prairie and looked out the sight for a city for the Saints, which was called Zarahemla."[5] Elias Smith, a cousin of Joseph Smith, recorded in his journal for 24 June 1839 the following: "Moved from Commerce to Lee County, Iowa Territory, and went on the farm bought of F. P. Blevins." 87 In his journal for 16 August 1840, he recorded the death of the Prophet's brother Don Carlos and noted that there was a "Conference at Zarahemla" on that day. "[6] These early references to the name of the Iowa settlement previous to March 1841 indicate that the Saints referred to it as Zarahemla long before the reve­lation in question. There is no indication in these early sources that this designation was based upon revelation or even that it was Joseph Smith's idea. This evidence suggests, rather, that the name did not originate with the March 1841 revelation and that the Lord was referencing a location already known among the Saints by that name. The purpose of the revelation was most likely to counsel the Saints to gather at the appointed place and not, as the authors [of the Heartland theory] suggest, to reveal the ancient location of a Book of Mormon city. The Saints did what they would often do—name places they lived after places mentioned in the Bible and the Book of Mormon. There is no compelling reason to associate the Iowa settlement with ancient Zarahemla

It is more like watching people build a model in real time and then get excited every time a piece seems to fit. That does not make the model worthless. Some of the methodology is genuinely reasonable: start with the text, prioritize physical geography over later traditions, pay attention to directions, rivers, seas, mountains, roads, and scale, and avoid forcing the text to mean something it does not say.

The strongest part of their approach is the “big rocks first” idea. If you are filling a jar, you put the big rocks in first, then the pebbles, then the sand. In this context, the “big rocks” are seas, rivers, mountains, wildernesses, directions, climate, and ore. Those features are harder to move around than things like city names, ruins, or later cultural artifacts. That is a reasonable way to build a geographic model. If the physical geography does not work, no amount of smaller details should rescue the model.

But the main weakness is that the episode often treats “this could fit” as if it is much closer to “this probably fits.” That is the key thing to watch for. The river could behave that way. The roads could be ancient highways. The basin could explain the “down” language. The ore could match the text. The seeds could matter that much. The ocean route could work. Each point may be interesting, but stacking a hundred “could be” arguments does not automatically turn them into proof. A stack of possibilities is still a stack of possibilities unless each piece has been independently tested against strong alternatives.

The episode also has a strong risk of confirmation bias. Once people begin to believe a model might be right, every new match starts to feel meaningful. The hosts repeatedly have moments like, “Holy cow, that’s there too,” or “we thought we had you, but then it lined up.” Those moments are emotionally compelling, but they are not the same thing as rigorous testing. It is like buying a new truck and suddenly seeing that same truck everywhere on the road. The truck did not magically become more common; your brain became tuned to notice it.

A related issue is the “treasure map problem.” If you already believe the treasure is buried in a certain field, every odd-shaped rock, old trail, dip in the ground, and rusty nail starts looking like evidence. Some of it may matter. But unless you define the test before you start digging, you can accidentally draw the target around whatever you find. In this episode, some details seem to have been predicted by the model, but others feel discovered after the fact and then absorbed into the model as confirmation.

Their treatment of roads is a good example. They make an interesting point about “highways cast up,” especially by comparing the phrase to biblical language about removing stones and clearing a way. That is a smart textual observation. But when they then see wide old roads in Baja, the question should not simply be, “Could these match the Book of Mormon?” The stronger question is, “How old are they, who built them, what were they used for, and do they uniquely match this text better than other explanations?” Without that extra testing, the roads are interesting, but not decisive.

The same thing happens with the river. Their argument about a shallow river, a basin, a head of the river, and bodies being carried toward the sea is one of the more thoughtful parts of the episode. But it still depends on several assumptions: that the text requires this exact kind of river, that the bodies literally traveled all the way to the sea, that “head” means a spring-fed pool rather than headwaters, and that this Baja river is the best match compared with other candidates.

The seed and ocean-route arguments are weaker because they carry the most hidden assumptions. You have to assume what kind of seeds they were, when they needed to be planted, how fragile they were, how they were stored, whether the group made landfall, what route they took, and how much the text intends us to infer from the seed detail. Thinking about winds, currents, seasons, and navigation is fair. But the episode moves quickly from “this route could work” to “this route makes the most sense.”

Another thing to watch is how they use humility language and confidence language at the same time. They often say, “this is just plausibility,” “this is an assumption,” and “we are not proving anything.” That is good. But then they also say things like “it fits,” “there isn’t a better model,” and “the details lined up.” Those are stronger claims. The safer version is, “Baja has some interesting textual correspondences worth testing.” The overstated version is, “the model has lined up so well that it is probably the place.”

There is also a noticeable inconsistency in how they treat outside assumptions. They criticize scholars or scientists for extrapolating from known Spanish introductions of date palms, but then they also extrapolate from the Book of Mormon narrative to explain date palms, seeds, ships, routes, animals, and roads. That does not mean their extrapolations are automatically wrong. But the real question is not “who is extrapolating?” Everyone is. The real question is whose extrapolation is better supported and more carefully tested.

One of the better insights in the episode is about scale. They are probably right to challenge the oversized mental image many readers have because of artwork, movies, and inherited assumptions. If the early Nephite group was small, it makes sense that early buildings, temples, walls, cities, and communities may have been much smaller and more practical than people imagine. That is a strong corrective. But correcting exaggerated assumptions does not automatically validate Baja. It only clears away one obstacle.

The best way to understand this episode is to see it as enthusiastic model-building, not a controlled courtroom case. They have gathered a lot of details that seem to point in the same direction, and some of those details are genuinely thought-provoking. But the episode does not fully test the model against the strongest alternative explanations. It mostly shows how Baja can absorb objections and turn many of them into possible confirmations.

That is why the sharpest critique is not, “This is ridiculous.” It is more precise than that: the episode has a thoughtful framework, but the execution often moves too quickly from pattern recognition to confidence. Pattern recognition is powerful, but it can also fool you. A constellation looks like a hunter, a bear, or a scorpion because the human mind connects dots. The real question is whether the dots were independently placed by the text in a way that uniquely points to Baja, or whether Baja is being used as the canvas on which the dots are being connected.

So the honest takeaway is this: the Baja model, as presented here, has some legitimate internal logic and several interesting textual-geographic observations. But many of its strongest-sounding moments are built on assumptions, live discoveries, emotional enthusiasm, and “could fit” reasoning. The strongest version of their case is careful, textual, and exploratory. The weakest version is when excitement turns plausibility into confidence faster than the evidence actually allows.

So the real question is how much of Book of Mormon geography are we wrongly trying to force onto a post-destruction landscape?

Videos like this may present interesting geography theories or possible parallels between ancient American sites and locations described in the Book of Mormon, but they do not constitute actual proof that the Book of Mormon is historically true. The arguments are largely based on interpretation and correlation — such as matching coastlines, travel distances, or terrain features — rather than direct archaeological evidence. No verified inscriptions, artifacts, writings, or DNA evidence have been discovered that conclusively connect ancient American civilizations to Nephites, Lamanites, or other Book of Mormon peoples.

While these videos can appear convincing visually, mainstream archaeologists and historians do not accept them as evidence that proves the Book of Mormon. Many of the proposed geographic models have changed over time, and similar land features can often be found in multiple locations. For something to qualify as strong archaeological proof, there would typically need to be direct and independently verifiable evidence, such as inscriptions with identifiable names, confirmed Hebrew or Egyptian writing systems tied to the narrative, or clear historical records connecting the civilizations to the Book of Mormon account. To date, that kind of evidence has not been found or accepted by the broader academic community.

Proof is a final, conclusive demonstration.

Selected Early LDS Teachings and Why They Raise Serious Concerns for Me

Joseph Smith — April 7, 1844 — King Follett Discourse

Before giving the King Follett Sermon, Joseph Smith essentially stated that if he taught false doctrine, then people would have the right to reject him as they would any false prophet. He challenged listeners to carefully examine his teachings and implied that accountability mattered when someone claimed to speak for God.

In that same sermon, Joseph Smith taught:

“God himself was once as we are now, and is an exalted man.”

He also taught:

“You have got to learn how to be gods yourselves.”

This became one of the foundational teachings behind eternal progression — the idea that God the Father was once a being like us, that Jesus Christ progressed through divine stages, and that humans can eventually become gods themselves.

Lorenzo Snow later summarized the doctrine this way:

“As man now is, God once was; as God now is, man may become.”

Brigham Young — April 9, 1852 — Journal of Discourses, Volume 1, pages 50–51

In the famous Adam–God sermon, Brigham Young taught:

“When our father Adam came into the garden of Eden, he came into it with a celestial body, and brought Eve, one of his wives, with him.”

He then declared:

“He is our Father and our God, and the only God with whom we have to do.”

Brigham Young also stated:

“Jesus, our elder brother, was begotten in the flesh by the same character that was in the garden of Eden.”

And further said:

“Jesus Christ was not begotten by the Holy Ghost.”

These teachings have historically been understood to mean that Adam was the literal physical father of Jesus Christ through Mary.

Yet later LDS prophets publicly denounced the Adam–God doctrine as false teaching. In 1976, President Spencer W. Kimball stated:

“We denounce that theory…”

That creates a serious issue for me. If Brigham Young taught these things publicly as prophet and mouthpiece of God, and later prophets denounced those teachings, then someone has to be accountable for those contradictions.

Orson Hyde — October 6, 1854 — Journal of Discourses, Volume 2, page 82

Apostle Orson Hyde taught that Jesus Christ was married, saying regarding the wedding at Cana:

“It will be discovered that no less a person than Jesus Christ was married on that occasion.”

Brigham Young — October 8, 1861 — Journal of Discourses, Volume 9, page 309

Brigham Young stated:

“The Scripture says that He, the Lord, came walking in the Temple, with His train; I do not know who they were, unless His wives and children.”

Early LDS leaders also repeatedly taught that exalted humans could eventually become gods, rule kingdoms or worlds, and continue eternal increase through spirit children and celestial marriage.

Another major concern for me is the Kirtland Safety Society.

Joseph Smith and other church leaders attempted to obtain a legal banking charter in Ohio, but were denied. Despite that denial, they continued operating under the name “Kirtland Safety Society Anti-Banking Company.” The word “Anti-Banking” itself acknowledged that it was not a legally recognized bank, yet it still functioned like one and issued notes.

Members invested real property, money, and assets into the institution because they trusted Joseph Smith and church leadership. Some leaders reportedly proclaimed that God and Jesus Christ had assured them the institution would succeed and not fail. Yet it collapsed, many members lost substantial resources, and Joseph Smith and Sidney Rigdon were later fined for illegal banking activity.

The same pattern appears again with the Happiness Letter connected to Joseph Smith and Nancy Rigdon. Joseph Smith publicly denied and concealed plural marriage for years, yet privately pursued secret relationships. The Happiness Letter contains statements such as:

“That which is wrong under one circumstance, may be, and often is, right under another.”

To me, this appears to be an attempt to persuade a young woman to accept a secret plural marriage arrangement through appeals to divine authority.

All of this leads me to the same conclusion: Jesus Christ is not the author of confusion, and Heavenly Father is not the author of confusion. Yet throughout LDS history, prophets speaking as the mouthpiece of Jesus Christ have taught doctrines that later prophets from the same church denounced, reversed, or distanced themselves from.

If Joseph Smith himself stated in the King Follett Sermon that people should hold him accountable if his teachings proved false, then why should later generations ignore that standard? If prophets claim divine authority from the pulpit, then members should absolutely have the right to examine those teachings and hold those prophets accountable when contradictions and false teachings emerge.

I suppose any of the proposed sites should be considered with this idea in mind - the climate, the geography, and the ruins have all changed with the passage of 2,500 + years.  We’ll never be sure of anything on this subject until we can talk to those who lived it…

Some things absolutely change over 2,500 years — cities disappear, rivers shift course, forests grow or vanish, coastlines can move, climate can fluctuate, and ruins deteriorate or become buried. But major geographic frameworks usually remain remarkably stable over that timeframe. Mountain ranges, large river valleys, narrow passes, peninsulas, seas, volcanic regions, travel corridors, and regional relationships often persist for tens or even hundreds of thousands of years.

So while we may never achieve absolute certainty about Book of Mormon geography from archaeology alone, geography still matters a great deal. The text describes a real physical world with constraints: directions, distances, elevation changes, travel times, seas, rivers, bottlenecks, climate zones, and population limits. Any proposed model should reasonably fit those internal geographic requirements.

Not a professional geologist, but as an amateur prospector, mineral collector, and in general geology enthusiast, I will challenge the notion that it's rare to find gold, silver, and copper together. While a mineral region is typically well-known more for producing one of the three as the primary commodity, many, if not the majority of places where you find one of those minerals, you will also find the others. Gold usually carries impurities of copper and silver, sometimes up to 50% silver. What makes a mine a "gold mine", a "copper mine" or a "silver mine" is not which minerals are found there, it's which mineral is found in a quantity and form that makes it profitable to mine, and perhaps relatively more profitable than the secondary minerals. Especially on a micro scale appropriate to an ancient context, deposit rich enough to mine by ancient methods will likely have gold, silver, and copper, in some quantity and would more likely have them in their native form. Gold is most commonly found in its native form. While silver and copper also occur natively, it's possible that the Nephites could have also recognized the much more common high-grade ores of copper and silver like malachite, and galena, and known how to process them. They would likely have different mines that were richer in one mineral or the other even if still in the same region. I'm not saying this is a problem for the theory, but it's not a smoking gun. Whatever maps or data were used to draw the conclusion were probably too high-level which doesn't help as much when trying to evaluate the viability of resources for a budding ancient civilization. Also, every ancient mine started with surface outcrop. Without modern exploratory drilling, there would be no reason otherwise to attempt an excavation. So the point about surface minerals vs mining is moot.

The biggest problem with a Baja setting is that it does not seem to fit the scale and complexity of the societies described in the Book of Mormon. Critics argue that the peninsula has weak archaeological support for dense urban life, large populations, major fortifications, and long-running state-level civilizations during the relevant time periods.

Main objections
Too little evidence of complex society. The Book of Mormon describes cities, kingdoms, trade, wars, records, and major population movements, but critics say Baja has not produced the kind of archaeological footprint you would expect from that level of civilization.

Sparse excavations are not the same as support. Baja has been less professionally excavated than some other regions, but that is an argument from absence of research, not positive evidence for the model.

Population size feels hard to place. Some reviewers say the number of people and the scale of battles in the text seem too large for what is known of Baja’s pre-Columbian history.

Cultural and linguistic fit is weak. One critique notes that the model lacks strong evidence tying Baja indigenous cultures or languages to the kind of civilization the text seems to describe.

Why complexity matters
Your instinct is basically right: if the Book of Mormon is read as a real ancient historical narrative, then the setting needs to plausibly support complex, organized societies with cities, agriculture, leadership structures, metallurgy, warfare, and writing. Critics of the Baja model say the peninsula’s known prehistory does not strongly match that profile.

@jaydavis1165 Yes, and I'll do it again. .Here you go: It only matches partly. A small initial colony can, in theory, grow quickly enough over time to create a much larger society, but the Book of Mormon also describes towns, cities, wars, kings, trade, records, and mass mobilization, so the real question is whether the urban logistics and environmental base can support that kind of civilization.

Population math
A “100 Lehites + 100 Mulekites” starting point is not automatically impossible. Population models used by Book of Mormon defenders argue that, with enough generations, even small founding groups can become large if growth is steady and if they absorb outside peoples.

But that does not solve the whole problem, because the text often implies much more than just headcount. It implies enough food production, settlement density, labor specialization, and political organization to sustain multiple centers of power and repeated warfare.

Urban logistics
This is where the criticism gets stronger. If there were large cities, armies, and sustained kingdoms, you would expect a supporting network of farms, roads, storage, water management, trade routes, and administrative centers. The issue is not only “how many people,” but “how did they live and move enough goods to make the society function?”

In other words, a population model can show that numbers might grow, but it cannot by itself show that the economy and settlement pattern are plausible.

Why Baja is hard
For Baja specifically, critics say the known archaeological record does not clearly show the kind of dense, complex urban system the Book of Mormon seems to require. So even if you reduce the population dramatically, you still have to explain where the cities, infrastructure, and state-level organization went.

That is why your objection is a strong one: lowering the population helps with one tension, but it does not fully answer the broader claims of the text about civilization scale and logistics.
